title: Strata giant unmasked in secret lobbying to save insurance kickbacks
full_text:
Australia’s largest strata company was granted secret permission by a government-backed review panel to privately lobby against a proposed ban on lucrative insurance kickbacks.
The Age can reveal that the sector’s biggest operator, PICA Group, privately warned the government’s expert panel that outlawing insurance commissions would cause 70 per cent of strata management companies to collapse.
The revelation comes after the Allan government stopped short of banning lucrative insurance kickbacks for strata managers in its highly anticipated response to a landmark review of the sector this week.
PICA’s intervention was kept from the public domain in a “confidential submission” to the review panel carried out by former Labor consumer affairs minister Marsha Thomson, economist Karen Chester and prominent strata lawyer David McKenzie.
However, the panel’s newly published report contains one reference in a body paragraph revealing PICA as the source of the 70 per cent claim. It attributes the figure to a “confidential submission” in a footnote – the only hint that the strata giant participated in the review at all.
PICA Group, Australia’s largest strata operator, oversees a national property footprint valued at $72 billion with a 10 per cent market share across 26 different brands of management companies and associated entities.
Among its biggest strata brands is Ace Body Corporate Management – the company at the centre of a leaked audio scandal published by The Age last week in which a senior manager was caught on tape attempting to solicit unlawful kickbacks from a supplier.
While the expert review states it received 159 written submissions, it only published 121 (including 53 anonymous) on the public-facing Engage Victoria website. This means 38 submissions were allowed to remain “confidential”, obscuring the identities and contents of their submissions – including PICA Group – from public scrutiny.
Public inquiries frequently allow for private submissions to enable the sharing of commercially confidential or otherwise sensitive information.
The expert panel also revealed a list of 53 organisations it had direct contact with on top of written submissions – 20 of which were redacted.
When questioned why PICA Group’s submission was granted confidentiality, a Victorian government spokesman said, “the review was conducted independently of government”.
Adam Promnitz, founder of the grassroots advocacy group Strata Owners Alliance, said it appeared there had been “classic closed-door lobbying from the strata industry’s big end of town”.
“Transparency matters, and the influence of the big end of town in strata should not be hidden from public view,” he said. “We disagree with the 70 per cent claim completely and have never seen credible evidence to support it. It feels like scaremongering to protect their interests.”
In a statement PICA Group denied it had attempted to conceal its position to the public, but declined to release its submission to The Age.
“PICA Group has been very open and public about our concerns regarding the prohibition of insurance commissions,” the company said in a statement. “We believe both consumers and the strata management sector would be worse off under such a change.”
PICA Group chief executive Bobby Lehane was formerly the chief executive of strata insurance company CHU underwriters.
The Age’s analysis of the 121 public submissions shows that the issue of insurance commissions was one of the most frequent and fiercely debated topics across dozens of responses.
The overwhelming majority of individual lot owners, volunteer committees, and consumer advocacy groups (such as Strata Owners Alliance, Mortgage Stress Victoria, Financial Counselling Australia, and Financial Counselling Victoria) demanded that the government implement an outright ban on the practice. Industry peak bodies and strata professionals (including the Strata Community Association Victoria, the National Insurance Brokers Association and several strata lawyers) strongly defended the right to continue collecting commissions.
Those in favour of a ban argue that allowing managers to take a percentage-based cut of insurance premiums creates a perverse incentive to secure the most expensive policies rather than the best deal for owners and that only a shift to a transparent “fee-for-service” model could restore trust in the sector.
The industry, however, argues that abolishing commissions would not necessarily lower insurance premiums for owners claiming managers would be forced to drastically increase their base management fees to cover the administrative costs of handling claims, ultimately shifting the financial burden back to lot owners anyway.
Hidden or undisclosed strata commissions are already illegal under Victorian law, however in its review the expert panel formally recommended going much further: recommending that any owners corporation manager be banned from receiving any kind of financial benefit from insurance products or any other kind of supplier – disclosed or not.
It referenced the Hayne Banking Royal Commission which found that “disclosure alone is often ineffective in addressing conflicts of interest and protecting consumers”.
The Allan government in its response listed that recommendation as “support in principle” but noted the industry’s warnings about the impact on strata businesses saying “a ban could potentially have strong market effects including an increase in the service fees OCs pay to professional managers and other market disruptions” and that “further work” was needed on the idea of a ban.
When pressed by The Age why such work needed to be replicated given the NSW Productivity and Equality Commission had just done such analysis finding ending commissions could generate more than $300 million in benefits, new Consumer Affairs Minister Paul Edbrooke said Victoria needed to do its own study.
Edbrooke would not commit to a timeline for completing a review.
“The changes introduced to parliament this week are the first of many reforms – and we’re not done,” a spokesman said.
Opposition consumer affairs spokeswoman Jade Benham said the government’s hesitation raised serious questions about its commitment to protecting residents.
“Secret commissions may be banned, but the broader question is whether commission and rebate arrangements are creating conflicts of interest that disadvantage lot owners and renters,” Benham said.
